coredata.dc:title: Controllable formation of polydopamine on carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase and ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay of benzocaine
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Shi-Qi
Jia, Bao-Zhu
Luo, Wei
Wang, Hong
Lei, Hong-Tao
Zhang, Wen-Feng
Xu, Zhen-Lin
Luo, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Sensing alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity with high sensitivity and accuracy is critical for both ALP-related health and food safety supervision and the development of ALP-triggered immunoassay platforms. Herein, an ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensing system based on the controllable formation of luminescent polydopamine and efficient quenching of carbon dots was proposed for the ALP activity assay, achieving quantitative detection in the range of 0.01–100 mU/L. Furthermore, this RF sensing system was integrated with an ALP-based ELISA platform to construct an RF-ELISA for benzocaine, a potentially abused anesthetic in edible fish, and ultrasensitive assay at the level of fg/mL was realized. This ratiometric strategy-based platform effectively shields various interferences through the self-calibration effect, thus providing more accurate and reliable quantification results. This study not only offers an efficient method for ultratrace detection of ALP and benzocaine but also proposes a universal platform for ultrasensitive detection of diverse targets in food analysis by replacing the recognition unit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Benzocaine
Ratiometric fluorescence
Immunoassay
Polydopamine
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ALP activity
Feasibility of the RF sensing system for ALP
Performance of the RF sensing system for ALP
Analysis of ALP in milk sample by RF sensing system
Ratiometric fluorescence ELISA for BZC detection
Analysis BZC of real sample by RF-ELISA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) HRTEM image of CDs. (C) Particle size distribution of CDs. (D) Fluorescence emission (Em) and excitation (Ex) spectra of CDs. (E) Full-range XPS spectrum of CDs. (F) The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of CDs. (G) The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of CDs. (H) The high-resolution O 1 s spectra of CDs. (I) FT-IR spectra of CDs.

(A/B) TEM image of the CDs/DA composite. (C) UV − vis absorption spectra of the sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of DA. (D) Fluorescence spectra of the sensor system in the presence of different concentrations of DA. (E) Quenching efficiency of different concentration of DA to the fluorescence intensity of CDs. (F) The fluorescence life-time of CDs in the absence and presence of DA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the RF sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of AA. (B) RF responses of RF sensing system against different concentrations of AA. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the RF sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of ALP. (D) RF responses of the RF sensing system against different concentrations of ALP.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the RF-ELISA in the presence of different concentrations of BZC (10-15 g/mL-10-6 g/mL, a-j). (B) The calibration curve of the RF-ELISA for the detection of BZC.

(A) PET induced quenching of CDs via self-polymerization of DA to form green-emitting PDA on CDs. (B) Schematic illustration of the principle of the RF sensing system for ALP. (C) Schematic illustration of the principle of the RF-ELISA for benzocaine.

head.ce:title: Controllable formation of polydopamine on carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase and ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay of benzocaine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity with high sensitivity and accuracy is critical for both ALP-related health and food safety supervision and the development of ALP-triggered immunoassay platforms. Herein, an ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensing system based on the controllable formation of luminescent polydopamine and efficient quenching of carbon dots was proposed for the ALP activity assay, achieving quantitative detection in the range of 0.01–100 mU/L. Furthermore, this RF sensing system was integrated with an ALP-based ELISA platform to construct an RF-ELISA for benzocaine, a potentially abused anesthetic in edible fish, and ultrasensitive assay at the level of fg/mL was realized. This ratiometric strategy-based platform effectively shields various interferences through the self-calibration effect, thus providing more accurate and reliable quantification results. This study not only offers an efficient method for ultratrace detection of ALP and benzocaine but also proposes a universal platform for ultrasensitive detection of diverse targets in food analysis by replacing the recognition unit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) have garnered considerable interest in disease diagnosis, food safety supervision and environmental analysis because of their cost effectiveness, good specificity, high throughput capacity, and ease of automation (Liao et al., 2021; L. Luo et al., 2022; S.-Z. Luo et al., 2022). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is commonly employed to label antibodies/antigens in ELISA for signal transduction and amplification since it has outstanding catalytic activity, a wide range of substrate specificities, is easy to conjugate to antibodies, and works well in mild conditions with good stability (Zhao et al., 2019). Additionally, ALP is a crucial hydrolase that is ubiquitously found in many living organisms. ALP catalyzes the dephosphorylation of diverse substrates in different tissues and enhances calcium and phosphorus digestion and absorption. Aberrant fluctuation of ALP activity is directly linked to a variety of illnesses, including liver dysfunction, bone damage, leukemia, prostate cancer, and lymphatic cancer (Wang et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2021). In addition, ALP activity is an important indicator for supervising the quality of milk, and the presence of ALP activity in milk above 0.35 mU/mL is indicative of inadequate pasteurization since ALP is more resistant to heat than the most thermotolerant microbe in milk, Mycobacterium paratuberculosis (Albillos et al., 2011). As a result, sensitive and accurate detection of ALP activity is crucial for ALP-related health and food safety monitoring, as well as the development of ALP-triggered immunoassay platforms. To date, various protocols have been developed to determine ALP activity, such as colorimetry (Qian Yang et al., 2020), fluorometry (Zhan et al., 2021), electroanalysis (Zhu et al., 2021), surface enhancement Raman scattering (Lin et al., 2022) and chemiluminescence strategy (Hai et al., 2017); nevertheless, advances in the sensitivity, precision and simplicity of ALP detection are still being pursued.

Fluorescence sensing strategies are particularly appealing and promising owing to their ease of use, high sensitivity and suitability for real-time detection, accounting for 58% of all enzyme test protocols (Lloyd, 2020).Notably, ratiometric fluorescence (RF) nanoprobes armed with two fluorescent signal indicators, which can minimize extraneous interference due to their self-correction capability, have aroused increasing research interest for more sensitive and accurate ALP detection in recent years (Cai et al., 2019; J. Chen et al., 2021; R. J. Wang et al., 2021; Q. Yang et al., 2020). However, most of the reported fluorescence sensing strategies were only suitable for detecting ALP with high activity in the range of tens to hundreds of units per liter (U/L), which might be applied for disease-related ALP assays in clinical samples. The highly sensitive RF strategy which is sensitive enough (health limit for ALP in pasteurized milk: 0.35 U/L) to analyze the residual ALP in pasteurized milk with a more complex sample matrix (compared to clinical samples), has rarely been reported. Yang et al. (Q. Yang et al., 2020) proposed an RF and colorimetry dual-mode nanoplatform for testing ALP in livestock and dairy products based on MnO2 nanosheets, dopamine and CdTe/ZnS QDs. However, the RF mode exhibits a quantification range from 4 to 80 mU/mL, which failed to allow the assessment of the adequacy of milk pasteurization. In fact, it is still highly desired yet difficult to design a simpler and more sensitive RF sensing strategy for detecting ALP.

Polydopamine (PDA) is a well-developed melanin-mimicking material typically formed from the self-polymerization of dopamine (DA) under oxidative stress or alkaline circumstances (Ma et al., 2016). In the past decade, PDA has attracted much attention in the fluorescence sensing field because of its striking biocompatibility, simplicity in synthesis, and tailorable photophysical properties. As an excellent electron acceptor and light adsorber in the ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared region (NIR), PDA can act as an efficient fluorescence quencher to quench diverse fluorophores, such as UCNPs (D. Wang et al., 2015), NIR-emitting fluorescent polymer dots (J. Wang et al., 2019) and carbon dots (CDs) (H. Li et al., 2020), via Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and/or photoinduced electron transfer (PET) in a fluorescence sensor. Meanwhile, the intrinsic and stable fluorescence emission of PDA also makes it an attractive fluorescence donor when designing fluorescence sensors (Ma et al., 2016). For instance, several fluorescent assays for some antioxidants (biothiols, ascorbic acid, Trolox) (D. Wang et al., 2015) and related enzyme analysis (M. Yang et al., 2018) have been developed by harnessing the inhibitive effect of various antioxidants toward the spontaneous polymerization of dopamine, which prevents the polydopamine-induced quenching effect. Yildirim and Bayindir reported a turn-on fluorescent sensing strategy for fast DA detection by measuring the intrinsic fluorescence of in situ-generated PDA from the self-polymerization of DA under alkaline conditions (Yildirim & Bayindir, 2014). Xiao et al. fabricated a fluorescent probe for ALP sensing by using PDA and manganese dioxide nanosheets as the energy donor and acceptor, respectively (Xiao et al., 2018). Although PDA is a kind of bifunctional material with both fluorescence emission and quenching ability, which is attractive for fabricating simplified RF nanoplatforms without the requirement of multiple labeling or modifications to probes, RF sensing strategies using these dual functions of PDA simultaneously have seldom been explored.

As a novel class of carbon nanomaterials, CDs possess intriguing properties, such as customizable emission wavelengths, excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and ease of synthesis, which make them promising candidates for biosensing, bioimaging and biomedicine (Ethordevic et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022; Yoo et al., 2022). In this work, blue-emissive CDs were employed as the response fluorophore, which can be efficiently quenched by the green-emissive PDA formed from the self-polymerization of DA under alkaline conditions (Scheme 1 A). However, when introducing ALP into the system, ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of 2-phospho-l-ascorbic acid (AAP) into ascorbic acid (AA), which then efficiently blocks the polymerization of DA, thus decreasing the fluorescence of the formed PDA and giving rise to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. As a result, an ultrasensitive RF sensor for the ALP activity assay in fresh milk was achieved (Scheme 1B).

Furthermore, the high sensitivity of the ALP-reporting system encouraged us to develop an ALP-based RF-ELISA using benzocaine (BZC) as a model antigen target. BZC is a topical anesthetic widely used in medical treatment (Insausti et al., 2020). In recent years, BZC has emerged as a potential anesthetic that might be illegally used to improve fish survival rates during long-distance transportation of fresh fish (Uehara et al., 2019). However, ingestion of aquatic products containing excessive BZC residues could be hazardous to human health. by causing some adverse effects, such as dermatitis, urticaria and angioedema (Pysarevska et al., 2018). Meanwhile, BZC residues can spread in water and soil, posing grave environmental risks (S. Chen et al., 2019). Thus, evaluating and monitoring trace levels of BZC is critical for protecting human health and controlling environmental contamination. In this study, the variation in BZC concentration was sensitively transduced into the change of RF signal with the aid of the corresponding anti-BZC monoclonal antibody (mAb) and ALP-labelled secondary antibody (ALP-Ab2), and an ultrasensitive RF-ELISA was developed for monitoring BZC residue in aquatic products (Scheme 1C). The excellent detection performance suggested that the proposed RF sensing platform holds great promise for analyzing a diverse array of targets in clinical diagnosis, food safety supervision, and environmental analysis with the help of corresponding antibodies.

ELISA plates were washed in a Multiskan MK2 microplate washer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired using an Evolution 300 spectrophotometer and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigations were carried out using an ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Spectra of fluorescence were collected on a SpectraMax i3x multi-function microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). Images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were captured by a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was collected with VERTEX 70v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were measured by a FLS900 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

Blue-emissive CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method (Mandal & Das, 2020). In brief, diammonium citrate (150 mg) and urea (200 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure water (6 mL). The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated for 7 h at 180 °C. After the mixture cooled to ambient temperature, the crude mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter to eliminate substantial impurities. Afterward, the reaction mixture was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 6 h with a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 800 Da). Finally, the CD solution was collected and kept at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: A series of concentrations of ALP solution (50 μL, in Tris-HCl buffer) and AAP (100 μg/mL, 50 μL) were mixed and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, DA solution (10 mmol/L, 50 μL) and CD solution (50 μL) were added into the above mixture solution. After a 40 min-incubation at 37 °C, the fluorescence intensities of CDs (F460) and PDA (F510) were recorded with excitation at 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The fluorescent intensity ratio (F460/F510) was employed to quantify ALP activity. Pasteurized milk samples were centrifuged against an ultrafilter (low molecular weight cutoff) to remove interfering reducing small molecules such as AA and then diluted 100-fold with Tris-HCl buffer for analysis by the RF sensor described above.

The 96-well microtiter plates were coated with BZC-OVA (100 μL/well) overnight at 4 °C and then washed twice with washing buffer. The plates were then blocked with blocking buffer (120 μL) at 37 °C for 3 h. After discarding the blocking solution, the plate was dried in the oven at 37 °C for 1 h and then stored at 4 °C until use. Subsequently, BZC standards at different concentrations (50 μL, in PBS buffer) were added, followed by the addition of anti-BZC mAb (50 μL). After a 40 min-incubation at 37 °C, the plate was washed five times. Next, ALP-Ab2 (100 μL) was added to each well and maintained at 37 °C for 30 min, followed by a washing step with washing buffer (five times). Afterward, AAP (100 μg/mL, 50 μL) was added at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, DA solution (10 mmol/L, 50 μL) and CD solution (50 μL) were added into the above mixture solution. Finally, F460 and F510 were measured under the same conditions as for ALP activity detection, after a 40 min-incubation at 37 °C. A calibration curve was constructed by plotting F460/F510 against the logarithm of BZC concentrations. Tap water was filtered by membrane filters and diluted 10-fold with PBS buffer for direct analysis by RF-ELISA. For shrimp and tilapia, 1.0 g of sample was homogenized and extracted with 1.0 mL of acetonitrile and vortexed for 2.5 min. The supernatant was mixed with purification agents (75 mg MgSO4, 30 mg PSA, 15 mg NaCl, 30 mg C18), vortexed for 2 min, and then centrifuged (3286 g, 2 min). Afterward, the supernatants were diluted 100-fold in PBS buffer for analysis by RF-ELISA. Moreover, analysis results of the RF-ELISA were validated by LC-MS/MS (Chinese industry standard method for determination of BZC residues in exported aquatic products, SN/T 5441–2022). The analysis work through LC − MS/MS was performed by Shenzhen Flux Detection Technology Co, Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). The parameters and conditions of LC − MS/MS are detailed in the Supplementary Materials .

First, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size of the CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1 A-C, the CDs had a homogeneous spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.3 nm and possessed a highly crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. Fig. 1D illustrates that the synthesized CDs exhibited an intense emission peak at 460 nm while being excited at 350 nm. XPS characterization was carried out to investigate the surface states and elemental composition of CDs. Full-range XPS of CDs revealed three distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 400.1 eV and 531.9 eV, which corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1E) (Ding et al., 2016). Three peaks representing sp2 carbons (CC/CC, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbons (CO/CN, 286.2 eV), CN (287.5 eV) and carboxyl carbons (CO, 288.9 eV) were deconvoluted from the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1F). Pyridine N (399.2 eV), pyrrole N (400.1 eV), and graphitic N (401.5 eV) were identified as the three peaks in the N1s band (Fig. 1G). Two peaks attributed to CO (531.5 eV) and CO (533.0 eV) were separated from the O1s band (Fig. 1H) (L. Luo et al., 2022). Surface groups on CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1I). The peak at 3430 cm−1 was ascribed to the amine group's NH stretching vibration, and its extension toward lower wavenumbers could be attributed to the OH stretching vibration. Additionally, peaks at 1620 cm−1, 1415 cm−1, and 1190 cm−1 were linked to the CO stretching vibration, OH bending vibration, and CO stretching vibration, respectively. FT-IR analysis indicated that there are –NH2, –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of CDs, which was in accordance with the XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the RF sensing system for ALP
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1B plots the fabrication process of the RF sensor which is based on the optical properties of CDs and PDA, of which the production was from DA self-polymerization controlled by ALP. To demonstrate the viability of the RF sensor for ALP, the polymerization of DA under alkaline conditions to generate luminescent PDA which possesses quenching ability to CDs, should first be confirmed. Previous studies have demonstrated that under aerobic and alkaline conditions, DA can self-polymerize into melanin-mimicking PDA which possesses high adhesive properties due to its outstanding reactivity with amine, sulfhydryl and phenol groups (Lee et al., 2007; J. Li et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2016). Therefore, the amine groups on the surface of CDs can provide anchoring sites for the deposition of PDA. As displayed by the TEM image in Fig. 2 A, DA spontaneously polymerized around CDs to form the CDs@PDA composite. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2B revealed the presence of high-crystallinity CDs in the interior of the CDs@PDA composite. With increasing DA concentration, the characteristic absorption of PDA gradually increases, accompanied by gradual darkening of the solution color (Fig. 2C), which further proves the occurrence of DA self-polymerization. Moreover, an emission peak at 510 nm was observed in the fluorescence spectrum of the incubated DA solution under excitation at 430 nm (red line, Fig. S6), and the addition of CDs to the DA solution induced significant quenching of the luminescence of CDs at 460 nm (green line, Fig. S6). In addition, when increasing the DA concentration, the emission peak of PDA at 510 nm progressively intensified, whereas that of CDs at 460 nm gradually weakened (Fig. 2D); thus, an RF response was generated. To investigate the RF changes triggered by DA, different concentrations of DA (0–1 mmol/L) were mixed with CDs in Tris-HCl buffer and incubated for 40 min. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching rate to CDs steadily rose as the DA concentration increased, and a good linear correlation was observed in a concentration range of 0.02–0.1 mmol/L (Fig. 2E). These results suggested that controlling the formation of PDA can be used to tune the system's RF response. Furthermore, we investigated the effect of the presence and absence of postpolymerized DA on the fluorescence lifetime of CDs. Fig. 2F shows that the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs/DA system was shorter than that of CDs, which means that dynamic quenching occurred between PDA and CDs. It has been reported that PDA can function as a potent electron acceptor for many photoexcited fluorophores to quench their fluorescence efficiently (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019). Thus, the quenching of CDs by PDA is most likely due to PET.

The inhibitory effect of antioxidants, such as AA, glutathione and cysteine, on the spontaneous oxidative polymerization of DA by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) oxidants has been demonstrated and used to construct antioxidant capacity sensing strategies (Ma et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). As depicted in Fig. S6, the addition of AA successfully blocked the oxidation of DA, resulting in a decrease in the emission intensity of PDA and the luminescence recovery of CDs (orange line). Additionally, the emission intensity at approximately 460 nm gradually increased with increasing AA concentration, but the emission intensity at approximately 510 nm steadily dropped. (Fig. 3 A). In particular, there was a good linear correlation between the RF signal and logarithm concentration of AA ranging from 0.0001 to 100 μmol/L, and the linear equation was F460/F510 = 1.83766 + 0.31301 Log [AA] (Fig. 3B). Since ALP can catalyze the dephosphorylation of AAP into AA, the addition of the combination of AAP and ALP caused the same fluctuation trend in the system's fluorescence spectrum as AA. (yellow line, Fig. S6). In summary, the above results indicated that this DA/CD-based RF sensing strategy could potentially be applied for ALP activity assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the RF sensing system for ALP
ce:section.ce:para: To attain the optimal analytical performance for ALP, several factors of the sensing platform, including AAP concentration, working buffer pH and preincubation time were optimized. The value of RF response (I460/I510) was used to indicate the performance of the proposed RF sensor. As the key substrate, sufficient amount of AAP directly determines the linear range and detection limit of ALP, whereas excessive AAP increase the detection cost. As seen from Fig. S7A, with the increase of AAP concentration, the RF response increased continuously and tended to level off after 80 μg/mL. Therefore, 80 μg/mL of AAP was chosen for subsequent experiments. Since the pH value has significant effect on the catalysis efficiency of an enzyme, and alkaline environment was beneficial to both the ALP-catalyzed enzymatic process and the polymerization of DA, the influence of the pH values ranging from 9 to 10.5 were investigated. Fig. S7B shows that the RF response reached a maximum as the pH value located at 9.8, which is consistent with the effective working pH for ALP suggested by ALP manufacturer (https://www.toyobo-global.com/seihin/xr/ lifescience/products). The effect of preincubation time on RF response to ALP cannot be ignored. Fig. S7C shows that the time required to attain steady RF response depends on the concentration of ALP to some extent. The presence of higher concentration of ALP means more DA are inhibited to polymerize into PDA, and less time is required to complete the self-polymerization reaction of DA. Therefore, the RF response levels off within 10 min in the presence of 10 mU/mL of ALP, while it took 40 min for RF response to level off when 1.0 mU/mL or 0.1 mU/mL of ALP is present. This incubation time is comparable to the time required for completing the self-polymerization of DA (5 mmol/L) under alkaline circumstances (D. Wang et al., 2015). To achieve a stable RF response to different concentrations of ALP, a 40 min-preincubation was employed for the further detection experiment.

Under the optimal conditions, the analytical performance of the RF sensor for ALP was investigated. Fig. 3C shows that the emission intensity of CDs at approximately 460 nm (F460) steadily increased, accompanied by a gradual decrease in the emission intensity of PDA at approximately 510 nm (F510) as ALP activity continuously increased. Notably, in an ultralow concentration range of 0.01–100 mU/L, the RF signal (F460/F510) was linearly correlated with the logarithm concentration of ALP. The linear equation was F460/F510 = 1.14995 + 0.30376 Log [ALP], mU/L (Fig. 3D). The limit of quantification for ALP was 0.01 mU/L, which was much lower than that of the commercial AttoPhos ALP fluorescent substrate-based sensing system (quantification range: 0.1 U/L-10 U/L, Fig. S8) and those of previously reported RF sensing strategies (Table S2), as well as the health limit (350 mU/L) for ALP in pasteurized milk.

To further verify the superiority of the RF sensor, its specificity, stability and accuracy were evaluated. As displayed in Fig. S9, the proposed RF sensor exhibited high selectivity to ALP against common proteins/enzymes, including AChE, BSA, choline oxidase, HRP, IgG and trypsin, since all of these proteins/enzymes could not induce obvious RF response as ALP did. Moreover, the stability of the assay based on the ratiometric signal of F460/F510 and the single emission signal of CDs (F460) or DA (F510) were compared. Fig. S10A shows that the value of F460/F510 remained almost constant, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs and PDA progressively decreased with prolonged detection time (within 40 min). After successive measurements of the same assay solution six times, the RSD of F460 and F510 was 20.8% and 20.5%, respectively, while the RSD of F460/F510 was only 0.9% (Fig. S10B). In addition, six parallel ALP standard solutions (0.1 U/L) were analyzed by the sensing system (Fig. S10C), the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the ratiometric strategy was 6.8%, whereas the RSD of the quantification results based on the single emission signal of CDs and PDA was 13.0% and 13.9%, respectively. These data indicated that the RF sensor can achieve higher stability and reliability than its single-emission-based counterpart.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ALP in milk sample by RF sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the RF sensor for potential application for residual ALP activity detection in pasteurized milk was also investigated. Ultrahigh-temperature processed milk was employed as the negative sample for spike and recovery tests. Thanks to the ultrahigh sensitivity of the RF sensor, it was found that the matrix effect can be suppressed to a negligible level by simply centrifuging the milk sample against an ultrafilter (low molecular weight cutoff) to remove potential reducing molecules (i.e., AA) combined with a 100-fold dilution with working buffer (Fig. S11). Therefore, the negative milk sample was spiked with ALP at three concentrations (0.01, 0.1 and 1 U/L) and analyzed by the RF sensor for the recovery test. As shown in Table S3, the mean recoveries were between 99.9% and 102.7% with RSDs below 3.9%. These data indicated that the RF sensing platform for ALP has good accuracy and applicability and can maintain good repeatability in the ultrasensitive detection range. The exceptional performance of this RF sensing platform could be primarily attributed to two distinctive features: (i) The multiexcitation format-based ratiometric strategy, which used the CDs/DA probe, had a built-in correction from external interference, which significantly improved the sensitivity and precision; (ii) the in-situ synthesis of PDA in this sensing system performed the multiple roles of AA recognizer, signal reporter, and nanoquencher, which not only provided potent quenching properties that enhanced the efficiency of photoinduced electron transfer, but also simplified fabrication and detection procedures, thus further improving the sensitivity and robustness of the RF sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence ELISA for BZC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasensitive CD/DA-based RF sensing system for ALP inspired us to further develop RF-ELISA for BZC detection by combining it with the anti-BZC mAb and ALP-based ELISA platform. Initially, a specific mAb against BZC was first produced by our group (see the Supplementary Materials ). With the assistance of the obtained anti-BZC mAb and commercial ALP-Ab2, we investigated the performance of the RF-ELISA to detect BZC. Scheme 1C depicts the combination of antigen/antibody pair-based competitive immunorecognition and the ALP-mediated RF response of the CD/DA system for the detection of BZC. In this strategy, coating antigen was first precoated on microtiter plates. When BZC was present, the coating antigen and BZC competed to bind with the anti-BZC mAb. Therefore, the amount of the formed coating antigen–antibody immunocomplex on the well was inversely correlated with the BZC concentration. After binding with ALP-Ab2, a signal path was generated, and then AAP/CDs/DA solution was added successively as a ratiometric fluorogenic substrate for signal amplification. The ALP labeled on Ab2 catalyzed the hydrolysis of AAP to AA, blocking the generation of PDA from DA. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 4 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 460 nm (F460) steadily dropped as the BZC concentration rose, whereas the fluorescence intensity of PDA at 510 nm (F510) gradually increased. The RF signal (F460/ F510) exhibited a linear relationship with the logarithm concentration of BZC ranging from 10-15 g/mL to 10-6 g/mL. The linear equation was F460/ F510 = 1.15788–0.21893 Log [BZC] (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.05 × 10-14 g/mL (S/N = 3), which is at least 4 orders of magnitude lower than that of conventional colorimetric ELISA (10-10 g/mL) using the same anti-BZC mAb. To evaluate the selectivity of the RF-ELISA for BZC, some structure- or function-related analogs were selected as controls. Fig. S12 shows that these potential interfering compounds (100 ng/mL) induce a negligible response in RF-ELISA compared to BZC (1.0 ng/mL), indicating the excellent selectivity of RF-ELISA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis BZC of real sample by RF-ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: We further validated its practicality by employing RF-ELISA to determine BZC in water, shrimp and tilapia samples. First, sample matrix should be evaluated and overcome. Since potential interference from fat, protein and other substances in a complicated food sample matrix, as well as the organic solvent (acetonitrile in this work) for extraction might significantly interfere with the interactions between antibody and antigen. To minimize matrix effects, the most efficient method, dilution, was applied after samples were pretreated as described above. Due to the relatively simple sample matrix, the tap water sample was just pretreated by filtering through membrane filters. Then, the RF response induced by different dilutions of tap water sample matrix in PBS buffer were assessed. As shown in Fig. S13A, with the increasing of dilution factors, the level of the induced RF response gradually approaches to that induced by PBS buffer. A dilution factor of 10-fold for tap water matrix was found induce negligible matrix effect on the RF-ELISA. For shrimp and tilapia samples, these samples were extracted with acetonitrile, and then the obtained sample extracts were purified by interacting with the purification agent. Subsequently, the purified sample extracts were directly diluted in PBS buffer with different dilution factors for the assay by RF-ELISA. As shown in Fig.S13B and C, the interferences from sample matrix gradually bated as the dilution factor increased. When shrimp sample extract and tilapia sample extract were diluted 100-fold (or higher dilution factor), the induced RF responses were at comparable level to that induced by PBS buffer. Thus, negative water, shrimp and tilapia samples (confirmed by LC-MS/MS) spiked with three different levels of BZC standard were subjected to the corresponding sample pretreatment steps and then analyzed by RF-ELISA to calculate the recoveries. Table 2 shows that the recoveries of the spiked BZC ranged from 94.1% to 102.5% with RSDs less than 4.2%. Moreover, the results of the RF-ELISA were in good accordance with those of LC-MS/MS, suggesting the potential practicability of RF-ELISA for detecting BZC in real samples.

In summary, we have developed an extendable RF sensing platform for the detection of ALP activity and BZC immunoassay with exceptional analytical performance. By integrating the ALP-mediated generation of AA from AAP, the inhibition of AA toward the in-situ formation of luminescent PDA from DA, and the efficient quenching ability of PDA to CDs, an ultrasensitive RF sensor for ALP activity detection was rationally developed. Furthermore, an ultrasensitive and reliable RF-ELISA was developed for monitoring BZC residues in aquatic products by employing the obtained anti-BZC mAb as the recognition unit, the commercial ALP-Ab2 as the signal transducing module, and the AAP/CDs/DA mixture as ratiometric fluorogenic substrates for signal amplification. Benefiting from the extraordinary sensing capability for ALP with an ultralow quantification limit of 0.01 mU/L, RF-ELISA exhibited an LOD at the fg/mL level for BZC detection. In addition, the RF sensing platform has been applied to assay ALP activity in fresh milk and monitor BZC residues in aquatic products with good accuracy and reliability, showing great promise for profiling a broad range of targets in food safety supervision with the assistance of suitable recognition units.

Shi-Qi Lin: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Bao-Zhu Jia: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Wei Luo: Data curation, Investigation, Resources. Hong Wang: Investigation, Resources. Hong-Tao Lei: Investigation, Resources. Wen-Feng Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Zhen-Lin Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Supervision. Lin Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2021A1515012422, 2023A1515030011), the Foundation for Distinguished Young Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong (2022KQNCX055), the Research Fund Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Measurement and Emergency Test Technology (KF2021004), the “Climbing Plan” Project of Guangdong College Students Science and Technology Innovation Cultivation Special Fund (pdjh2021b0368).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136582.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
